I fully agree with the chosen option B, i.e., maintaining the delivery room temperature at 28-30°C for neonates. This temperature range effectively balances preventing both hypothermia and hyperthermia, two major risks facing premature babies whose thermoregulatory mechanisms may still be developing. Additionally, recent meta-analyses confirm that maintaining neonates' core body temperatures close to but slightly higher than the lower end of this spectrum—around 36.5°C—is linked with reduced mortality rates, fewer infections, and less need for respiratory support compared to extremes outside this range. Thus, the supportive evidence remains robustly aligned with clinical standards and patient welfare objectives. Answer Choice: **B**.
